perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the Province of British Columbia and the federal laws of Canada applicable therein.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the Province of British Columbia and the federal laws of Canada applicable therein. This clause is for informational purposes only and does not create any legally binding obligations.
perturbation.explanation: By adding a disclaimer suggesting the governing law clause is not legally binding, the agreement creates a contradiction regarding which laws actually govern the contract. This undermines the enforceability of the entire agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The common law principle of 'Contra proferentem' states that ambiguous terms in a contract should be interpreted against the party who drafted them.
perturbation.law_explanation: The added text introduces ambiguity to a crucial clause about governing law. The principle of 'Contra proferentem' dictates that this ambiguity would be interpreted against the drafter, making the intended governing law less enforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: contra proferentem | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contra proferentem Contra proferentem is a rule of contract interpretation that states an ambiguous contract term should be construed against the drafter of the contract. The term contra proferentem is derived from a Latin phrase meaning “ against the offeror .” Contra proferentem has become increasingly important with the rise of contracts of adhesion . Contracts of adhesion involve pre-written contracts which are offered on a strict take-it or leave-it basis, leaving no opportunity for a party to bargain over specific contractual terms. Because the party does not have an opportunity to negotiate, they may reasonably interpret a term in a different manner than the contract offeror intended. Contra proferentem exists to place the burden of ambiguity on the party most capable of mitigating that ambiguity - the person who wrote it. The doctrine of contra proferentem is also especially important in the field of insurance law due to the generalized nature of many of its terms. For example, it may be unclear if a policy that covers “water damage” will cover damage caused by a rainstorm induced mudslide. The doctrine of contra proferentem encouraged insurance providers to create enumerated lists of events that are excluded under a given policy, ultimately increasing clarity for insurance purchasers. [Last reviewed in July of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords contracts Wex COMMERCE commercial activities consumer protection insurance LIFE EVENTS standards of tort liability tort damages financial events THE LEGAL PROCESS courts legal practice/ethics business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions individual rights legal education and practice legal theory Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified contract includes a clause that negates the legally binding nature of the governing law provision. This is a structural flaw leading to a legal contradiction. The addition stating the clause is 'for informational purposes only and does not create any legally binding obligations' directly contradicts the original intent of establishing which laws govern the agreement. The principle of 'Contra proferentem', as defined in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that ambiguous contract terms should be construed against the drafter. By disclaiming the legally binding nature of the governing law clause, the contract introduces ambiguity, making the intended governing law less enforceable and creating a significant legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient agrees that, in the event of any breach of any provision hereof, the Discloser may, or will, not have an adequate remedy in money or damages. The Recipient therefore agrees that, in such event, the Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against such breach in any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient agrees that, in the event of any breach of any provision hereof, the Discloser may, or will, not have an adequate remedy in money or damages. This clause is for informational purposes only and does not create any legally binding obligations. The Recipient therefore agrees that, in such event, the Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against such breach in any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: By adding a disclaimer suggesting the equitable relief clause is not legally binding, the agreement creates a contradiction regarding the possibility of equitable relief. This undermines the enforceability of the claim to injunctive relief.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract law regarding the availability of remedies for breach of contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: The added text introduces a disclaimer into the remedies section of the contract, creating uncertainty around the availability of remedies and undermining the contract's ability to provide adequate relief for breaches, contradicting fundamental contract law principles about remedies for breach.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a statement that the equitable relief clause is 'for informational purposes only and does not create any legally binding obligations'. This directly contradicts the original text, which indicates that the Discloser 'shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief'. By suggesting the clause has no legal weight, the modification undermines the availability and enforceability of remedies for breach, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of confidentiality and restricted use contained herein will survive for a period of five (5) years from the date of receipt of any Confidential Information from the Discloser under this Agreement .
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of confidentiality and restricted use contained herein will survive for a period of five (5) years from the date of receipt of any Confidential Information from the Discloser under this Agreement . The Parties agree that the survival period is for illustrative purposes only and not a binding term.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the survival period is "illustrative" and "not a binding term," the agreement contradicts itself, creating uncertainty about the actual duration of confidentiality obligations. This could render the confidentiality provisions unenforceable after the initial term.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract law principle that material terms must be definite and certain.
perturbation.law_explanation: The added text creates ambiguity about a material term - the duration of confidentiality. This makes the term indefinite and uncertain, potentially rendering it unenforceable under general contract law principles requiring definiteness of material terms.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 2-204. Formation in General. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-204. Formation in General. (1) A contract for sale of goods may be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement , including conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of such a contract . (2) An agreement sufficient to constitute a contract for sale may be found even though the moment of its making is undetermined. (3) Even though one or more terms are left open a contract for sale does not fail for indefiniteness if the parties have intended to make a contract and there is a reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a five-year survival period for confidentiality. The changed text adds that this period is 'illustrative' and 'not a binding term.' This directly contradicts the original clause by negating the defined survival period, thus creating uncertainty about the duration of confidentiality obligations. The Law Explanation provided reinforces this by stating that the added text creates ambiguity about a material term - the duration of confidentiality - potentially rendering it unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be executed by facsimile or other electronic means , in one or more counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original and all of which taken together shall constitute one and the same instrument .
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be executed by facsimile or other electronic means , in one or more counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original and all of which taken together shall constitute one and the same instrument . This clause is subject to change without notice.
perturbation.explanation: The added phrase 'This clause is subject to change without notice' creates uncertainty regarding the validity of execution through facsimile or electronic means. It implies that the method of signing the contract could be altered unilaterally, potentially invalidating already signed versions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common law principles regarding the requirements for valid contract formation, including mutual assent and certainty of terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: The added phrase introduces the possibility of unilateral change to the execution clause, which undermines the mutual assent requirement for valid contract formation. If the method of execution can be changed without notice, there's no guarantee that all parties agreed to the same terms at the time of signing, contradicting fundamental principles of contract law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The addition of 'This clause is subject to change without notice' introduces a structural flaw creating a legal contradiction because it undermines the mutual assent required for a valid contract. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that a contract requires 'mutual assent, expressed by a valid offer and acceptance' to be legally enforceable. The added phrase allows for unilateral changes to the method of execution, meaning parties may not be agreeing to the same terms at the time of signing. This contradicts the fundamental principle of mutual agreement, as one party could alter the execution terms after the other has signed, creating an unenforceable agreement. Because the original terms could be changed at any moment, it creates a structural flaw that undermines the contract, thus creating a legal contradiction.
